planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–November. Nellie Stevens is dioecious — plant a male holly nearby for berry production. Edward J. Stevens (the male counterpart) or any other male Ilex x meserveae or Ilex opaca male holly within 50 feet will pollinate. Without a male pollinator, no berries form. Trees begin producing berries 3–4 years from planting and become increasingly ornamental as they mature. Tolerates pruning well and can be sheared into a formal hedge or left to develop its natural pyramidal form.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil; Nellie Stevens Holly is a hybrid Ilex cultivar (believed to be Ilex aquifolium x Ilex cornuta) that has become one of the most popular evergreen screening trees and specimen hollies for the American South and transition zones — combining the large size and dense pyramidal form needed for privacy screening with exceptional heat tolerance and adaptability to Gulf South conditions. The large, glossy, dark green leaves with spiny margins create an impenetrable, formal-looking screen or hedge that requires minimal maintenance to maintain its naturally tidy pyramidal shape. In winter, heavily pollinated female trees are covered with large clusters of orange-red berries that persist through winter and are an important food source for robins, cedar waxwings, and other berry-eating birds. The combination of year-round ornamental value (evergreen foliage + winter berries), fast growth rate (2–3 feet per year in good conditions), and Gulf South adaptability has made Nellie Stevens one of the most planted ornamental trees across zones 6–9 for the past several decades.
sources: University of Georgia Extension — Nellie Stevens Holly for Southern Landscapes
North Carolina State University Extension — Holly Selection Guide
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Evergreen Screening Plants for Gulf South

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–November. Kaleidoscope is compact (2–3 feet) and rarely requires pruning to maintain its tidy mounding habit — remove any all-green reversion shoots immediately to prevent them from overwhelming the variegated growth. The seasonal color change is most dramatic: spring growth emerges golden-yellow, summer foliage is green-and-yellow variegated, and fall coloring turns the leaves orange, red, and burgundy before winter. In zone 9 this multi-season color display makes Kaleidoscope one of the most ornamentally valuable dwarf landscape shrubs available.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Kaleidoscope tolerates a wide range of conditions and is drought-tolerant once established; Kaleidoscope is a multi-season variegated abelia cultivar that earned recognition through All-America Selections and other awards for its exceptional year-round ornamental value — a compact (2–3 feet) mounding shrub that changes color through the seasons from spring's golden-yellow new growth through summer's bright green-and-yellow variegation to fall's spectacular orange, red, and burgundy tones. Small, tubular, white to pale pink fragrant flowers appear continuously from spring through fall, adding a floral dimension to the primarily foliage-based ornamental value. Abelia is one of the most butterfly-attractive landscape shrubs available — the small, tubular, nectar-rich flowers are visited by virtually all butterfly species present in zone 9 from spring through fall, making Kaleidoscope an effective pollinator garden plant that is also ornamental enough for formal landscape use. The compact habit, multi-season interest, and tolerance of partial shade make Kaleidoscope one of the most versatile small shrubs for Gulf South landscapes.
sources: University of Georgia Extension — Abelia Varieties for Southern Landscapes
North Carolina State University Extension — Abelia grandiflora Cultivar Guide

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown plants in spring; in zone 9 plant February–May or September–October. Trailing Rosemary is less cold-hardy than upright varieties — tolerating temperatures to about 10°F compared to 0°F or below for the hardiest upright types — but in zone 9 this is rarely relevant. The trailing, spreading habit creates an excellent ground cover for slopes, walls, and sunny dry areas. Prune lightly after bloom to maintain shape and prevent woodiness. In zone 9 rosemary is an evergreen perennial shrub that may live for decades in well-drained locations.

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil — rosemary rots in heavy, wet soil and thrives in conditions that would kill most other herbs; Prostratus is the trailing, prostrate form of rosemary (Salvia rosmarinus, formerly Rosmarinus officinalis) that cascades naturally over walls, slopes, and raised planters rather than growing upright — a different growth habit from the shrubby upright rosemary but with identical culinary flavor and fragrance. The woody, spreading stems reach 4–6 feet across while remaining only 1–2 feet high, creating an extensive ground cover with the characteristic needle-like, intensely aromatic rosemary leaves and lavender-blue flowers that appear in late winter and spring. Trailing Rosemary is particularly useful in zone 9 landscapes for covering slopes and dry, sunny, hard-to-maintain areas where conventional ground covers fail — the combination of extreme drought tolerance, deer resistance, salt tolerance, and attractive year-round appearance makes it one of the most practical landscape plants for these challenging conditions. The culinary quality is identical to upright rosemary: harvest sprigs of the strong, resinous, pine-and-camphor-scented leaves for roasting meats, flavoring breads, making herb oils, and the full range of Mediterranean herb applications.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Rosemary for Florida Landscapes and Herb Gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Rosemary Ground Cover for Gulf Coast
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Herb Production for Gulf South

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm (70°F+); in zone 9 plant March–May. Honeydew requires the longest growing season of any common melon (90–110 days) and the most heat to develop full sugar. Unlike cantaloupe, honeydew does not 'slip' from the vine when ripe — check for ripeness by skin color (cream to white-green), slight giving when pressed at the blossom end, and a characteristic sweet aroma. In zone 9 the heat of June through August is ideal for honeydew sugar development; consistent moisture during fruit development then reduced irrigation as fruits approach maturity produces the sweetest flesh.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil — honeydew requires warm soil, abundant heat, and consistent moisture during fruit development; Honey Dew is the standard honeydew melon variety — a winter melon type (Cucumis melo, Inodorus group) producing large (5–8 pound), oval to round fruits with a distinctive smooth, creamy-white to pale green skin that develops a subtle gold or cream color at full ripeness. The flesh is pale green, firm but juicy, with a mild, clean, very sweet flavor that is quite different from the musty, complex, aromatic sweetness of cantaloupe — honeydew's sweetness is cleaner and more neutral, making it the preferred melon for many people who find cantaloupe flavor too strong. Honeydew's longer shelf life and later harvest timing compared to cantaloupe made it historically important as a late-summer melon that could be stored and shipped where cantaloupe could not. Zone 9 is ideal for honeydew production — the extended summer heat and long growing season align perfectly with honeydew's need for consistently warm conditions over a long development period.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Honeydew Melon Production
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Honeydew Melon for Florida Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Honeydew and Winter Melon for Gulf South

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–November. Reifler's Dwarf is a compact selection (4–6 feet) compared to the species which can reach 15–18 feet, making it manageable for landscape use. Walter viburnum grows naturally in wet to moist, often flooded, acidic soils along stream banks and wetland margins throughout the coastal plain — it is one of the most flood-tolerant ornamental shrubs available. Cross-pollination with another Walter viburnum or selection is needed for berry production; plant two or more specimens for fruit.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist to wet, acidic to slightly acidic soil — Walter viburnum naturally grows in the wet, acidic soils of coastal plain wetlands and is highly tolerant of flooding and poor drainage; Reifler's Dwarf is a compact cultivar of Walter Viburnum (Viburnum obovatum), a native Florida and Gulf Coast evergreen viburnum that provides exceptional ecological value as a wildlife food source while offering ornamental merit in difficult, wet, or shaded landscape conditions. The small, rounded, dark green leaves and compact habit create a dense, fine-textured evergreen shrub suitable for hedging, screening, and naturalistic plantings in the challenging moist-to-wet soil conditions where many landscape plants struggle. In late winter and early spring (February–March), Walter viburnum produces flat clusters of small white flowers that attract early-season pollinators before most other landscape shrubs begin blooming. The small, red-to-black fruits that mature in summer are eaten by dozens of bird species — making Walter viburnum a high-value wildlife plant for zone 9 landscapes designed to support native birds. The native provenance means Walter viburnum is adapted to the exact soil, climate, and pest pressures of the Gulf South without the cultural challenges of introduced alternatives.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Viburnum obovatum Florida Native Plant
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Viburnum obovatum Native Landscape Value
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Native Shrubs for Gulf South Landscapes
